articles.title: US and Iran report 'positive progress' at Doha talks, agree to regroup after Khamenei funeral
articles.summary: Qatar's foreign ministry said indirect US-Iran talks in Doha, mediated by Qatar and Pakistan, made 'positive progress' on the 14-point memorandum of understanding covering the Strait of Hormuz, Iran's frozen assets, and the Lebanon ceasefire. A senior Iranian official said the sides reached an understanding on releasing a first $3 billion tranche of frozen Iranian funds held by Qatar, to be used by Iran's central bank for humanitarian purchases rather than transferred as cash; US officials disputed that characterization. The core dispute remains Hormuz sovereignty: Iran insists it and Oman have joint authority to administer the strait and charge passage fees once the 60-day MoU period ends, while Washington says any new arrangement needs Gulf states' endorsement. Both sides agreed to reconvene 'at the earliest possible time' after Iran's state funeral for assassinated Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, scheduled across Tehran, Qom and Mashhad from July 4-9.

articles.title: Prominent Netanya rabbi Amos Guetta stabbed to death at yeshiva; suspect arrested
articles.summary: Rabbi Amos Guetta, head of the Rabbi Yitzhak Hai Taieb Yeshiva in Netanya and a leading figure among Libyan Jewry, was stabbed four times and killed following morning prayers on Wednesday. Police say an argument broke out between Guetta and a man in his 20s who had come to pray with him; the suspect, described as known to police and reportedly mentally unstable, fled after also allegedly attempting to stab the rabbi's assistant. He was arrested shortly after. Medical teams found Guetta without a pulse at the scene and he was pronounced dead at Laniado Hospital. The killing was one of three fatal stabbings in Israel within about an hour on Wednesday, prompting commentary about a rise in violent crime.

articles.title: Netanyahu declines to rule out renewed Jewish settlement in Gaza
articles.summary: Asked about renewing Jewish settlements in Gaza, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said 'the question is whether you prefer to do or to talk,' adding 'yes, I prefer not to address it' rather than deny the prospect outright. The remarks come as nearly all of Gaza's 2.1 million residents remain displaced, compressed into shrinking areas as Israel expands the territory it holds; officials continue to link Mossad chief Roman Gofman to a Palestinian 'voluntary migration' plan from Gaza, though this is officially denied as being under his direction, with no final decision announced on who will lead the effort.

articles.title: Israel's renewed Gaza 'voluntary migration' push raises ethnic cleansing concerns
articles.summary: Middle East Monitor's weekly Palestine roundup examines Israel's renewed promotion of 'voluntary migration' of Palestinians out of Gaza, framing it as a mechanism that could amount to ethnic cleansing under international law. The piece also covers concerns that the Trump-proposed Gaza governance board could create legal exposure for participating officials and contractors, an unprecedented lawsuit filed by ICC judges against US sanctions targeting the court, and continued scrutiny of Israeli conduct toward journalists in Gaza, where press-freedom groups say a record number of reporters have been killed.

articles.title: IDF says it killed Hamas platoon commander and four other operatives in Gaza strikes
articles.summary: The IDF said a Tuesday airstrike in southern Gaza killed Adel Jihad Mohammad Asfour, a Hamas military-wing platoon commander it said had directed sniper and explosive-device attacks on Israeli troops and was working to rebuild Hamas capabilities and plan 'imminent' attacks. The military also named four other Hamas operatives — two anti-tank fighters, a second platoon commander, and a sniper — killed in strikes over the preceding week. Separately, Energy Minister Eli Cohen said Israel is increasing its control of Gaza and 'will not allow Hamas to recover' after the war. Hamas has not issued a public response to the specific claims.

articles.title: Syrian Airlines to resume Damascus-Amsterdam flights July 2, first European route since sanctions era
articles.summary: Syria's General Authority of Civil Aviation and Air Transport announced Syrian Airlines will resume direct Damascus-Amsterdam flights starting July 2, the flag carrier's first Western European route in more than a decade of political and regulatory isolation. The move follows the EU's February 2025 suspension of sanctions barring Syrian Arab Airlines from EU airports, and comes alongside a broader normalization of Syria's aviation links; Kuwait Airways separately resumed Damascus flights this week for the first time in 14 years.

articles.title: Coalition advances Basic Law shielding Haredi draft evaders from sanctions
articles.summary: Israel's governing coalition advanced a Basic Law intended to shield ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) men who evade military conscription from sanctions, reigniting a long-running domestic political fight over draft equality. The move came the same day former IDF chief of staff Gadi Eisenkot and former prime minister Naftali Bennett publicly disputed Netanyahu's claims about Iran's nuclear program, with Bennett accusing the prime minister of trying to 'rewrite history.' Separately, the 22nd Maccabiah Games opened in Jerusalem with roughly 5,000 athletes, including about 2,000 from Israel and 900 from the US.
